text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 12 February 2018 

Dr Wang
Saffron Park Hospital

Saffron Park, London, E14 7JL
Dear Dr Wang
John Doe, DOB: 21-11-1982, Hospital No: P546484 NHS No. 5484656746
Flat 15 Roundwood Road, London, E14 7JL 

I reviewed the above patient in the Neurology Clinic today. He is a rotating shift worker, including nights, and reports that his circadian rhythm is frequently disrupted by abrupt schedule changes and overtime. He has a confirmed diagnosis of focal epilepsy, likely frontal onset on prior outpatient EEG with anterior quadrant interictal spikes; MRI brain has been structurally unremarkable in the past. He previously suffered from frequent clusters of myoclonic jerks, absence seizures and occasional convulsive events 1 or 2 times per year, but is currently in long-term remission, having been seizure free for years. Despite erratic work hours he has had no clinical seizures, and he and his partner deny any nocturnal events, tongue biting, or injuries. 

He is maintained on Lamotrigine 200 mg b.d. with good tolerability; he notes occasional headaches after extended night shifts and some daytime somnolence that he attributes to sleep debt rather than medication. No rashes, diplopia, or ataxia reported. He rarely uses Melatonin 2 mg m.r. on transition weeks between night-to-day rotations (self-initiated after pharmacy advice); we discussed keeping usage minimal to avoid morning grogginess. 

From an occupational perspective, he describes sequences of three consecutive 12-hour night duties followed by rapid flip back to early shifts, which he finds challenging. He keeps a structured sleep diary and uses dark-out blinds and earplugs to consolidate daytime sleep. He avoids alcohol and maintains hydration. He carries his rescue midazolam oromucosal 10 mg but has not required it for several years. 

On examination today, he was alert and oriented, speech fluent, cranial nerves intact, no focal neurological deficits; gait normal. Blood pressure 124/78, pulse 68 regular. No cutaneous rash. 

Plan:
- Continue Lamotrigine 200 mg b.d. as current regimen is effective. 
- Provide an employer-facing letter outlining reasonable adjustments: predictable rota cycles where feasible, avoidance of rapid flip shifts, and protected rest periods. These measures are intended to reduce sleep deprivation, which he finds can precipitate auras historically, although none have occurred in years. 
- Routine monitoring: repeat U&Es and LFTs at next GP review in 6–12 months; no immediate bloods today as he is well and stable. 
- Safety counselling reinforced regarding driving regulations (he meets current standards with long-term remission), medication adherence, and avoiding missed doses during shift changes. 
- We discussed that if headaches worsen, we could consider sleep clinic input or actigraphy to optimise shift adaptation; he prefers to defer for now. 

Follow-up in 9–12 months, or sooner if there is any breakthrough event, new focal aware auras, adverse effects, or concerns. 

He agreed with the above plan. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: currently in long-term remission, having been seizure free for years

He previously suffered from frequent clusters of myoclonic jerks, absence seizures and occasional convulsive events 1 or 2 times per year, but is currently in long-term remission, having been seizure free for years

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states the patient is currently in long-term remission and has been seizure free for years. It also notes no clinical seizures despite erratic work hours, and no nocturnal events. The requirement for 'seizure free' is at least 6 months; 'years' clearly exceeds this. Therefore, the appropriate normalized label is 'seizure free for multiple years.' No need to calculate other frequencies because historical past seizures are superseded by current long-term remission.
seizure_frequency_number: currently in long-term remission, having been seizure free for years
reference: He previously suffered from frequent clusters of myoclonic jerks, absence seizures and occasional convulsive events 1 or 2 times per year, but is currently in long-term remission, having been seizure free for years

flaws.description: Gold (seizure free for multiple year) disagrees with Reference (seizure free for multiple month)
